title: Unit 02 Pretest
description: Diagnostic pretest for Career Research and Decision-Making.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of researching career information?

choices.text: To learn about job roles, responsibilities, and requirements
choices.feedback: Correct. Career research helps students learn specific facts about job roles, responsibilities, and requirements.

choices.text: To choose a career without comparing options
choices.feedback: Career research is meant to support comparison, not avoid it.

choices.text: To skip learning about training pathways
choices.feedback: Training pathways are an important part of career research.

choices.text: To focus only on hobbies instead of careers
choices.feedback: Career research should stay focused on career information.

questions.prompt: A certification is one type of preparation that may be connected to a career pathway.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: A certification can be part of a career pathway because it shows training or skill in a specific area.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Certifications can be an important part of education and training pathways.

questions.prompt: Which example best shows job outlook information?

choices.text: A description of what a worker does every hour
choices.feedback: That describes job duties, not job outlook.

choices.text: A list of school clubs a student enjoys
choices.feedback: School clubs are not job outlook information.

choices.text: Information showing whether a career is expected to grow or decline
choices.feedback: Correct. Job outlook information shows future demand or growth trends for a career.

choices.text: A summary of a student's favorite classes
choices.feedback: Favorite classes may connect to career interests, but they are not outlook data.

questions.prompt: A student is comparing two careers and wants to make an informed decision. What should the student do?

choices.text: Choose the first option without reviewing any evidence
choices.feedback: An informed decision should be based on evidence, not a quick guess.

choices.text: Ignore job duties and pathway requirements
choices.feedback: Job duties and pathway requirements are important when comparing careers.

choices.text: Pick the option a friend likes most
choices.feedback: A friend's opinion is not enough evidence for career decision-making.

choices.text: Compare information such as role, training, outlook, and personal fit
choices.feedback: Correct. Informed decisions come from comparing clear evidence across more than one factor.

questions.prompt: Technical training is one possible pathway students can research for a career.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Technical training is one possible education and training pathway for many careers.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Technical training can absolutely be part of a career pathway.

questions.prompt: What does salary information help a student understand?

choices.text: Which career cluster is most popular at school
choices.feedback: Popularity at school is not the purpose of salary information.

choices.text: How much workers may earn in a career
choices.feedback: Correct. Salary information helps students understand possible earnings in a career.

choices.text: Whether a student likes group projects
choices.feedback: That is a personal preference, not salary information.

choices.text: Which teacher gives the easiest assignments
choices.feedback: Salary data is about careers, not classroom assignments.

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes an informed career choice?

choices.text: A choice based only on one quick impression
choices.feedback: An informed choice should include more than one quick impression.

choices.text: A choice based only on salary
choices.feedback: Salary matters, but informed choices should include more than one factor.

choices.text: A choice based on comparing evidence and reflecting on options
choices.feedback: Correct. Informed career choices use research evidence and reflection together.

choices.text: A choice made without looking at training requirements
choices.feedback: Training requirements are an important part of career decision-making.

questions.prompt: Which factor belongs in evaluating career choices?

choices.text: Ignoring all research once a career sounds interesting
choices.feedback: Research should still be used even when a career sounds interesting.

choices.text: Avoiding any comparison between options
choices.feedback: Comparing options is part of evaluating career choices.

choices.text: Choosing based only on what someone else recommends
choices.feedback: Other opinions may help, but evaluation should include the student's own research.

choices.text: Looking at evidence from different sources before deciding
choices.feedback: Correct. Evaluating career choices means reviewing evidence before making a decision.
